behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of the values in cells A1 through A10
behaviors.justification: The SUM function requires coordinated action: the formula parser must recognize the function and extract the range syntax A1:A10, the range resolver must map this to actual cells, and cell values must be retrieved. A unit test of a raw sum() function would miss failures in range parsing, range-to-cell mapping, or value retrieval. Mocking cell values hides real failures in how ranges are identified and resolved. The failure boundary crosses the parser, range resolver, and cell value lookup components.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with cells A1 through A10 containing values 1–10. In cell A11, enter =SUM(A1:A10) and verify it evaluates to 55. Repeat with different ranges and value sets (negative numbers, decimals, zero, very large numbers) to ensure range syntax parsing and value retrieval work correctly across variations.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result
behaviors.justification: Cascaded formulas require the engine to identify dependencies, evaluate formulas in dependency order, and combine results. If you mock B1's value, you hide the failure that B1 itself failed to evaluate. The real boundary is across the parser, dependency resolver, and evaluator. The bug manifests as incorrect final values when intermediate formulas are evaluated in the wrong order or not at all. Testing requires multiple interdependent formulas to verify the full evaluation chain.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with A1=5, B1=A1*2 (formula), C1=B1+1 (formula). Verify C1 evaluates to 11. Test deeper chains (3–4 levels, e.g., A1→B1→C1→D1→E1) with different operations (multiplication, division, string concatenation) to ensure formulas are evaluated in correct dependency order and intermediate results propagate.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping
behaviors.justification: Circular dependency detection only triggers during live formula evaluation—you cannot test this at unit level. The failure (infinite loop or stack overflow) occurs when the evaluator traverses the dependency graph. A unit test of the detection algorithm in isolation would not exercise the real code path that causes runaway computation. The real boundary is the full evaluation pipeline with actual formula interdependencies and the cycle detection logic integrated into it.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with A1=B1+1 (formula) and B1=A1+1 (formula). Attempt to evaluate either cell and verify: (a) the engine returns a circular reference error, (b) no infinite loop or stack overflow occurs, (c) error includes context identifying the cycle. Test longer cycles (A→B→C→A, A→B→C→D→A) and self-referential cycles (A1=A1+1) to verify the detection algorithm handles arbitrary cycle lengths and patterns.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP requires parsing the formula syntax, evaluating arguments, resolving the table range into cells, and searching within actual data. Mocking the lookup table defeats the test—you cannot verify search behavior without real cells. The real failure could occur in range resolution, column-index handling, search algorithm, or error handling for missing keys. All these pieces must work together; the failure boundary spans the parser, range resolver, and search logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table in columns A:B, rows 1:5 (product names and prices). In a separate cell, enter =VLOOKUP("Product X", A1:B5, 2, 0) and verify it returns the correct price when found. Create a second formula with a non-existent product and verify it returns #N/A error. Test with different column indices (col 1, col 2, col 5), case sensitivity, empty cells in the table, and edge cases (first row, last row) to ensure range resolution and search work correctly.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"))
behaviors.justification: Nested IF functions require the parser to handle recursive function call syntax, the evaluator to correctly assess all conditions and select branches in the right order, and proper type coercion for comparisons and return values. While IF logic is simple, the real failure is in how the parser chains nested calls and the evaluator processes them. A unit test of IF in isolation would miss parsing failures or incorrect branch selection in nested contexts.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with A1=5, and enter nested formula =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"). Verify it returns "low". Test with A1=15 (expect "medium") and A1=25 (expect "high"). Test deeply nested conditions (3+ levels), mixed return types (numbers, strings, cell references, formulas in branches), and edge cases (zero, negative, large numbers) to ensure parsing and branch evaluation work correctly at all nesting depths.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE's correctness depends on correctly parsing arguments, retrieving values from cells (which may be empty, null, zero, or blank strings), and joining them according to rules about skipping empty cells. Mocking cell values would hide subtle differences between empty string, null, and uninitialized cell states. The failure boundary spans the parser, cell retrieval logic (which must distinguish cell types), and the concatenation logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells: A1="Hello", A2=(empty), A3="World", A4="!". In B1, enter =CONCATENATE(A1,A2,A3,A4) and verify result is "HelloWorld!" (A2 skipped, no blanks inserted). Test with: (a) cells containing numbers, text, mixed types; (b) leading and trailing empty cells; (c) nulls vs. empty strings; (d) zero-value cells; (e) formulas in arguments to ensure arguments are evaluated before concatenation.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require the parser to recognize sheet-qualified syntax (Sheet!Cell), the sheet registry to locate the correct sheet object, and the cell resolver to find the target cell within that sheet. The real failure could occur at any step: syntax parsing, sheet lookup, or cell retrieval. Testing requires multiple sheets and actual formula evaluation across sheet boundaries; mocking would hide sheet resolution failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with two sheets: Sheet1 and Sheet2. In Sheet2, set A1=10. In Sheet1, create formula B1=Sheet2!A1+1 and verify it evaluates to 11. Test: (a) referencing non-existent sheets (should error); (b) non-existent cells (should error); (c) chains across multiple sheets (Sheet1 references Sheet2 which references Sheet3); (d) circular cross-sheet references to ensure sheet resolver works correctly in all cases.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order
behaviors.justification: Correct recalculation requires: identifying which cells depend on which, determining the topological order, and recalculating in that order. If formulas are calculated out of order, a formula might use a stale value from a dependency that hasn't been recalculated yet. The failure boundary spans the dependency tracker, evaluator, and recalculation orchestrator. Testing requires multiple interdependent formulas and verifying that changes propagate correctly in the right order.
behaviors.test_description: Create: A1=5, B1=A1*2 (formula), C1=B1+3 (formula), D1=A1+C1 (formula). Verify initial values: B1=10, C1=13, D1=18. Change A1 to 7 and verify recalculation: B1=14, C1=17, D1=24. Test: (a) deeper chains (5+ cells); (b) diamond dependencies (multiple paths from one cell to another); (c) verify no cell is recalculated more than once; (d) verify order is deterministic and correct under repeated changes.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values
behaviors.justification: Undo requires: capturing complete state before the change (formula text and all dependent values), restoring everything atomically, and ensuring no partial undos. The real failure could be: undo only restores formula but not values, only some dependent values, or the undo state is incomplete. Testing requires setting up formulas with dependencies, changing one, and verifying that undo restores all components correctly. Mocking the undo mechanism would hide incomplete state capture.
behaviors.test_description: Create: A1=5, B1=A1*2 (formula), C1=B1+1 (formula). Verify B1=10, C1=11. Edit B1 to =A1*3. Verify B1=15, C1=16. Trigger undo and verify: (a) B1's formula is restored to =A1*2; (b) B1's value returns to 10; (c) C1's value returns to 11. Test: (a) undo/redo cycles; (b) multiple consecutive edits with reverse undo; (c) undo with non-contiguous dependent cells; (d) recovery from partial state corruption to ensure undo log captures all necessary state.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires file I/O—writing to disk and reading back. The real failure occurs at the serialization/deserialization boundary: binary encoding/decoding errors, floating-point precision loss, formula string corruption, or file I/O errors. These cannot be caught without actual disk I/O; mocking file operations hides format and I/O bugs. The test must verify the saved/loaded state byte-for-byte (for data) and value-for-value (for computed results).
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with mixed cell types: integers (42), floats (3.14159265359), strings with special characters, formulas (=A1+B1), cross-sheet references. Save to disk in the engine's native binary format. Load into a fresh instance. Verify: (a) all cell values match exactly (including floating-point precision to full bits); (b) all formula text is identical; (c) all dependent formulas re-evaluate to the same results; (d) cross-sheet references still resolve. Test with: large workbooks (1000+ cells), edge cases (empty sheets, cells with very large/small numbers, Unicode strings, special characters, preserved circular references in saved state).
